cataloging_subject.label: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-defined selection of Middle English source texts represented by the U-M item aha2688 from Richard Morris's First Series: twenty-nine numbered print containers, with the complete Nero version of the Orison of God treated as a witness version of the incomplete Lambeth item rather than a second abstract work, and with the separately headed Lesser Creed counted as an additional intellectual work. The selected appendices preserve parallel witness versions, not new works. This cataloging unit is not a single medieval work, one manuscript, the complete 1868 publication, its omitted modern-English translations and apparatus, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors
catalog_summary.date: Component works and versions chiefly twelfth–thirteenth centuries; edited selection issued 1867–1868
catalog_summary.region: England; the surviving witnesses represent several regional and dialectal contexts

titles.value: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloging from Morris's title page and series statement

assertion.notes: Morris's title uses Old English in nineteenth-century usage; the represented vernacular texts are cataloged here as Early Middle English.

titles.value: Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises (Sawles Warde, and þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd: Ureisuns of Ure Louerd and of Ure Lefdi, &c.) of the Twelfth and Thirteenth Centuries

scope.description: The medieval works and versions assembled in Morris's selection

assertion.notes: The anthology contains multiple anonymous works. Shared dialect, manuscript context, or traditional grouping does not establish one author for the whole selection.

scope.description: Selection and arrangement of the numbered texts, translations, notes, and appendices
The printed Middle English text, with witness-specific dependencies and George Parker's working contribution recorded in lineage
The modern-English translations in the 1868 print edition, which the U-M XML explicitly omits
The Bodley-based Sawles Warde working transcript supplied to Morris, including the Royal terminal supply

assertion.notes: This responsibility is routed through a provisional historical working-transcript entity in lineage; it does not imply that the checked-in XML was transcribed directly by Parker.

scope.description: Royal marginal readings and selected Cotton Titus D XVIII readings supplied for Morris's Sawles Warde text and notes
Broad date range for the component Middle English works and versions, not one composition event

assertion.notes: The component works and surviving copies have distinct dates; witness production dates are recorded in lineage and are not automatically work-composition dates.

scope.description: Broad cultural origin of the component Middle English works

assertion.notes: The witnesses have different production localizations; this broad work-level statement does not collapse those physical contexts into one composition site.

scope.description: The selected vernacular homilies, prayers, poems, and devotional prose
Biblical and liturgical lemmata, quotations, formulae, and rubrics embedded within the Middle English works

form_statements.description: Twenty-six represented intellectual works are prose-only and three are verse-only. Parallel appendix versions and witness-specific paratext are excluded from the intellectual-work form totals.

scope.description: The dominant genre of the anthology; not every numbered prayer or poem is itself a homily

subject_statements.label: Prayer and devotion to Christ and the Virgin Mary
The soul, sin, judgment, and salvation

summaries.value: Richard Morris's First Series brings together anonymous Early Middle English homilies, doctrinal expositions, prayers, devotional prose, and religious poems from seven principal manuscript witnesses. Its subjects include the liturgical year, penitence, the Creed and Pater Noster, moral conduct, death and judgment, devotion to Christ and Mary, guardianship of the soul, and the wooing of Christ. The U-M representation preserves the Middle English source-text stream but not the facing modern-English translations.

Seventeen numbered Lambeth homiletic, doctrinal, and verse pieces; the Lambeth and Egerton versions of Poema Morale; an incomplete Lambeth and a complete Nero version of an Orison of God; a Marian poem; prayers and praises from Nero; four Vespasian homiletic pieces; Sawles Warde from Bodley with Royal supply and collation; þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd from Titus; and selected parallel-text appendices from Egerton and Royal.

The cataloging unit follows the represented source-text selection. It separates twenty-nine print containers, twenty-eight XML DIV2 elements, and twenty-nine intellectual works: XML division N=20 merges printed XX and XXI; XXI is a complete witness version of the incomplete work printed as XIX and therefore adds no new abstract work; the separately headed Lesser Creed within XXIII does add one. Appendix I and Appendix III are parallel witness versions, and the Royal colophon and nine-line prayer after Sawles Warde are witness paratext.

extent.description: The cataloged selection has twenty-nine numbered print containers but twenty-nine intellectual works only after version and nesting boundaries are applied. Printed XXI is a complete witness version of XIX and contributes zero new works; the separately headed Lesser Creed nested under XXIII contributes one. The intrinsic verse totals are 305 lines in seven groups for VI, 396 lines in one group for XVIII, and 171 lines in one group for XX. Raw XML totals are eleven LG and 1,153 L because they also count the 272-line Egerton parallel Moral Ode and the nine-line Royal witness prayer after Sawles Warde. The thirty-three modeled sections are the twenty-nine numbered containers, the Lesser Creed, the Royal witness paratext, and the two represented appendix versions.

content_parts.label: I. In Dominica Palmarum
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily for Palm Sunday from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: II. Hic dicendum est de Quadragesima
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily on Lent from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: III. Dominica prima in Quadragesima
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily for the first Sunday in Lent from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: IV. In diebus dominicis
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily for Sundays from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: V. Hic dicendum est de Propheta
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily concerning the prophet from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: VI. Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse exposition of the Lord's Prayer from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: VII. Credo
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose exposition of the Creed from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: VIII. De Natale Domini
content_parts.summary: Incomplete Middle English prose discourse on the Nativity from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487; Morris points to a probable beginning printed in his Second Series.

content_parts.label: IX. In die Pentecosten
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose Pentecost homily from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487, identified by Morris as an adaptation of Ælfric.

content_parts.label: X. De octo viciis et de duodecim abusivis huius seculi
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily on eight vices and twelve abuses from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487, identified by Morris as an adaptation of Ælfric.

content_parts.label: XI. Dominica V Quadragesimæ
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily for the fifth Sunday in Lent from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XII. Dominica secunda post Pascha
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily for the second Sunday after Easter from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XIII. Sermo in Epistolam II ad Corinthios IX.6
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose sermon on 2 Corinthians 9:6 from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XIV. In die Dominica
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose Sunday homily from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XV. Sermo in Marcum VIII.34
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose sermon on Mark 8:34 from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XVI. Estote fortes in bello
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily on spiritual warfare from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XVII. Sermo in Psalmum CXXVI.6
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose sermon on Psalm 126:6 from Lambeth Palace Library MS 487.

content_parts.label: XVIII. Poema Morale
content_parts.summary: The Lambeth version of Poema Morale, continued from Egerton MS 613 where the Lambeth copy breaks off.

locators.value: ff. 60r–65r, followed by an Egerton continuation

content_parts.label: XIX. On Ureisun of Oure Louerde
content_parts.summary: Incomplete Lambeth witness version of an Orison of God; the complete Nero version is printed separately as XXI.

content_parts.label: XX. On God Ureisun of Ure Lefdi
content_parts.summary: Middle English Marian devotional poem from Cotton MS Nero A XIV.

content_parts.label: XXI. Þis is on wel swuðe god Ureisun of God Almihti
content_parts.summary: Complete Nero witness version of the Orison represented incompletely by XIX. The U-M/XML stream is itself partial: it omits the heading and opening at Morris p. 200 / Nero f. 124r and begins at p. 201 / f. 124v.
notes: Zero item count prevents a second witness version from inflating the abstract-work total; it does not mean the print container is empty.

content_parts.label: XXII. On Lofsong of Ure Lefdi
content_parts.summary: Middle English praise or prayer to the Virgin from Cotton MS Nero A XIV.

content_parts.label: XXIII. On Lofsong of Ure Louerde
content_parts.summary: Middle English praise or prayer to Christ from Cotton MS Nero A XIV; Morris's print container also ends with a separately headed Lesser Creed.

locators.value: ff. 128v–131r; exact Lesser Creed locus unresolved

content_parts.label: Þe Lesse Crede
content_parts.summary: Separately headed Middle English Creed text printed within container XXIII.
notes: The XML carries a legacy f. 131v milestone, while the current BL catalog describes Latin Apostles' Creed material at ff. 131r–v. Exact language and locus reconciliation remains open in lineage.

content_parts.label: XXIV. De initio creature
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose homily from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII, identified by Morris as a transliterated and slightly abridged Ælfrician homily.

content_parts.label: XXV. An Bispel
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose exemplum or homiletic piece from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII.

content_parts.label: XXVI. Induite vos armatura Dei
content_parts.summary: Short Middle English prose homiletic piece from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII.

content_parts.label: XXVII. Dominica IIII post Pentecosten
content_parts.summary: Short Middle English prose homily for the fourth Sunday after Pentecost from Cotton MS Vespasian A XXII; Morris tentatively associated it with an Ælfrician homily.

content_parts.label: XXVIII. Sawles Warde
content_parts.summary: Middle English devotional prose allegory, copied principally from Bodleian MS Bodley 34 with its lost ending supplied from Royal MS 17 A XXVII and collated against Royal throughout.

locators.value: terminal supply through f. 10v; collation throughout

content_parts.label: Royal witness colophon and prayer
content_parts.summary: Royal MS 17 A XXVII's Johan scribal colophon and a nine-line prayer retained after Sawles Warde.
notes: Witness-specific paratext, not counted as another abstract work or as intrinsic Sawles Warde verse.

content_parts.label: XXIX. Þe Wohunge of Ure Lauerd
content_parts.summary: Middle English devotional prose from Cotton MS Titus D XVIII.

content_parts.label: Appendix I. A Moral Ode, Egerton MS 613
content_parts.summary: Parallel Egerton witness version of Poema Morale, included to print material omitted from the Lambeth copy and support comparison.
notes: Zero item count marks a parallel witness version of part XVIII, not an empty appendix.

content_parts.label: Appendix III. Her cumseð þe oreisun of seinte Marie
content_parts.summary: Incomplete Royal MS 17 A XXVII parallel witness version of part XX, On God Ureisun of Ure Lefdi.
notes: Zero item count marks an incomplete parallel witness version, not an additional abstract work.

related_works.title: Poema Morale
notes: Part XVIII prints the Lambeth copy, completed from Egerton; Appendix I prints the fuller Egerton witness version. These are versions of one work, not two abstract works.

related_works.title: Homilies of Ælfric used as sources for selected Middle English adaptations
notes: Morris identifies IX and X as transliterations or adaptations of Ælfric and XXIV as a transliterated and slightly abridged Ælfrician homily. His association of XXVII with an Ælfrician homily is explicitly tentative and is not promoted to a confirmed source relation.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2688.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for U-M and edition identifiers, title and imprint, encoded selection boundaries, numbered divisions, headings, text, page and folio milestones, verse structure, notes, appendices, and OCR/encoding declarations.

evidence.citation: Structural XPath, print-page, folio-milestone, line, and hash inspection of aha2688.xml.
evidence.locator: counts and per-division inspection of DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, HEAD, P, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, and NOTE
evidence.summary: Reproducibly establishes twenty-eight DIV2 elements for twenty-nine numbered print containers, the merged XX/XXI division, omission of XXI's p. 200 opening, 1,153 raw lines in eleven groups, 872 intrinsic lines in nine groups, selected appendix boundaries, and the distinct Lesser Creed and Royal witness paratext.

evidence.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series, EETS Original Series 29 and 34 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1868), complete First Series within Internet Archive item oldenglishhomili00morruoft.
evidence.locator: First Series PDF pp. 5–398; title page; preface pp. v–xv; numbered source text on odd printed pp. 3–287; Appendix I on printed pp. 288–295; Appendix III on p. 305
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title, imprint, two-part issue, contents, print containers, edition order, text boundaries, apparatus, translations, appendices, and the distinction between the target First Series and the separately bound Second Series in the 710-page scan carrier.

evidence.citation: Richard Morris, Preface and manuscript/source notices in Old English Homilies and Homiletic Treatises, First Series (1868).
evidence.summary: Morris's direct statements identify the anthology as a bundle of fragments and smaller treatises; distinguish the Lambeth, Nero, Vespasian, Bodley, Royal, Titus, and Egerton dependencies; credit George Parker's Bodley transcription and collations; and identify confirmed and tentative Ælfrician relationships.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item AHA2688.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for title, 1868 publication, digital identity, public access, collection rights assertion, and the current rendered text.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for aha2688.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence separating the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Morris edition, bound scan carrier, working transcript and collations, seven manuscript witnesses, complete Egerton viewer, access routes, rights layers, exact source relations, and separately modeled U.S. Copyright Office duration guidance used only for rights analysis.

open_questions.question: Which modern work records, dates, localizations, and source identifications should replace or qualify Morris's nineteenth-century descriptions for every anonymous component?
next_steps: Review modern specialist editions and repertories component by component.
Preserve witness-specific versions and explicit uncertainty rather than assigning one date, author, or dialect to the anthology.

open_questions.question: What keying, OCR, image-link, and revision history lies between the 1868 copy and U-M's 2006 Level-1 TEI representation?
next_steps: Obtain U-M production documentation beyond the inherited XML header.
Compare any current downloadable U-M XML bitstream with the checked-in artifact.

notes: The record preserves three separate counts: twenty-nine numbered print containers, twenty-eight encoded DIV2 elements, and twenty-nine intellectual works.

Printed XXI is the complete Nero witness version corresponding to the incomplete Lambeth Orison printed as XIX. Its U-M stream is partial because the XML begins at Morris p. 201 / Nero f. 124v and omits p. 200 / f. 124r and the heading.
Morris's title term Old English is retained as bibliographic evidence; Early Middle English is the descriptive language category used for the vernacular works.

Modern-English translations, introduction, much apparatus, Appendix II, and back matter remain outside the represented text selection even though the complete First Series scan is available in lineage.
